---
pagetitle: "Triangulating Traces for Humanitarian Response"
page-layout: full
toc: false
title-block-style: none
---

```{=html}
<div class="hero-banner compact">
  <h1>Triangulating Traces for Humanitarian Response</h1>
  <div class="subtitle">Combining satellite imagery, surveys &amp; socioeconomic data to measure the impact of man-made disasters</div>
  <div class="meta">
    <span>Elisabetta Pietrostefani · University of Liverpool</span>
    <span>SPACERAISE 2026 · Week 3 · GSSI, L'Aquila · 19 May 2026</span>
  </div>
</div>
```

## About this training

This training is part of the **SPACERAISE 2026 Doctoral School** module on *Geospatial Data and Quantitative Analysis in Social Science* at the Gran Sasso Science Institute (GSSI), L'Aquila, Italy.

It covers how to combine **satellite remote sensing data** with **traditional survey data** to measure the impacts of man-made disasters — illustrated with the case of the **2020 Beirut Port Explosion**.

## Schedule for May 19

| Time | Session |
|------|---------|
| 08:30 – 10:00 | **Lecture** · Triangulating Traces for Humanitarian Response |
| 14:00 – 15:30 | **Lab** · Geo data lab: Man-made disasters (Beirut case study) |

## What you will learn

By the end of this training you will be able to:

-   Understand how **satellite damage proxy maps** are constructed from pre/post imagery
-   Spatially join and compare **remote sensing**, **expert structural assessments**, and **ground-truth survey** data
-   Run and interpret a simple **regression model** linking physical destruction to a socioeconomic outcome
-   Critically assess where different data sources agree — and where they diverge

## Materials

| Page | What it covers |
|------|----------------|
| [Lecture slides](lecture.qmd) | Conceptual background on 3 case-studies |
| [Setup & data access](lab_setup.qmd) | Download the data, set up R, troubleshooting |
| [Datasets reference](datasets.qmd) | What each file contains and how to read it |
| [Part 1 — Satellites & destruction](lab_part1.qmd) | Measuring damage from MAXAR imagery |
| [Part 2 — Satellite vs surveys](lab_part2.qmd) | How the satellite measure matches Copernicus, JRC, and household reports |
| [Part 3 — Damage, sentiment & economic stress](lab_part3.qmd) | One regression linking damage to income — then student exploration |
| [Pipeline appendix](pipeline_demo.qmd) | The full Random Forest pipeline, illustrated (not run in the session) |
| [From grids to areas](satellites_to_areas.qmd) | How satellite imagery becomes area-level statistics (Imago context) |

## Case study: Beirut Port Explosion (August 4, 2020)

The explosion at the Port of Beirut was one of the largest non-nuclear explosions in history, equivalent to approximately 1–1.5 kilotons of TNT. It devastated large parts of the city and provides a compelling case study for how we can use multiple data types — satellite imagery, expert structural assessments, household surveys, and socioeconomic indicators — to understand the spatial patterns of destruction and recovery.

::: callout-note
## Data note

This lab uses **MAXAR / WorldView satellite-derived damage data**, the **Copernicus EMSN087** building-level structural assessment from the EU Joint Research Centre, and a **synthetic version of a household survey** modelled on a real in-person survey conducted in Beirut post-blast. The synthetic data preserves the statistical structure of the original while protecting respondent confidentiality.
:::
